items.title: Approximating a smooth regression curve on [0, 1]
items.scenario: A factory sensor maps temperature x in [0, 1] to efficiency y = sin(2πx) + 0.1x. A single-hidden-layer MLP with 32 tanh units can closely match the curve on that interval after training — illustrating that non-linear targets are within an MLP's expressive class.
items.explanation: UAT says such an approximation is possible in principle with enough units; training may succeed with modest width on this smooth 1-D function. Test on held-out x values to check generalization — UAT alone does not certify that.

items.title: XOR boundary — why one linear layer fails
items.scenario: XOR labels are not linearly separable in the original (x1, x2) plane. A network with one hidden layer of ReLU units can carve two half-planes and combine them to classify XOR, while a linear model cannot.
items.explanation: This is the classic motivation for hidden layers: non-linear activations enable non-linear decision boundaries. UAT generalizes this idea to arbitrary continuous functions on compact sets, not just XOR.

items.title: Wide shallow vs narrow deep on the same task
items.scenario: On MNIST, a shallow MLP with one hidden layer of 512 units vs a deeper stack 256→128→64 can reach similar test accuracy. The deeper net often uses fewer total parameters for comparable error because layers build hierarchical features.
items.explanation: UAT permits the wide shallow solution; depth is a practical efficiency and optimization choice. Neither architecture is prescribed by the theorem — validation accuracy and compute guide the pick.

items.title: Perfect training fit, poor test performance
items.scenario: A student trains a 10,000-unit single-hidden-layer net on 500 noisy tabular rows until training MSE ≈ 0. Training plots look perfect; validation MSE is 10× higher.
items.explanation: The network class is expressive enough to memorize noise (UAT-compatible) but generalization failed. This is overfitting — addressed by regularization, more data, or simpler models, not by citing UAT.
